SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are supported by a realistic empirical evaluation (spaceship physics tasks) with appropriate baselines (reactive, iterative, metacontroller) and ablations over expert types (MLP, IN, true simulator) as described in Sections 3 and D.2. The technical approach for training (actor-critic for controller/memory, REINFORCE + entropy for manager, use of a critic when f is non-differentiable) is coherent and spelled out (Sec. 2.3 and Appendix B), and experts are trained with standard supervised losses (Appendix B.1). There are some methodological caveats acknowledged by the authors — e.g., convergence variability for large ponder steps, entropy regularizer causing the manager to over-ponder, and limited improvements when using multiple experts (Sections 3.3 and D.3) — and no statistical significance tests are reported; these are minor issues that do not invalidate the main conclusions but do limit confidence in some detailed claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper brings together metareasoning and learned internal simulation in a single learned metacontroller that jointly chooses how many internal simulations to run and which expert to consult, which is a meaningful extension beyond prior works cited (Horvitz & Russell on metareasoning, Graves' adaptive computation, Schmidhuber, and recent learned optimizers). The formulation of an RL-trained manager that trades off task loss and computational cost (Eq. 3 and the meta-level MDP in Appendix A.1) and the empirical study over heterogeneous experts is a non-trivial contribution. While the building blocks are drawn from existing literature, the particular integration and empirical demonstration of adaptive expert selection and per-instance computation allocation is novel enough to be of interest.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (Intro → Model → Experiments → Detailed Appendices) and key components are clearly described with algorithms (Algorithms 1–3) and formal definitions (e.g., LP, LR, LT in Sec. 2.2 and meta-MDP in Appendix A.1). Notation is mostly consistent and implementation/training details are thorough (Appendix D), enabling reproducibility. A minor clarity issue is some repetition/formatting artefacts near the end of the provided text (TeX remnants and duplicated paragraphs), but they do not obscure the main exposition.
